The three deities that first appeared were: *{{Nihongo|[[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]|天之御中主神}} - Central Master *{{Nihongo|[[Takamimusubi]]|高御産巣日神|1=Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} - High Creator *{{Nihongo|[[Kamimusubi]]|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami}} - Divine Creator A bit later, two more deities came into existence: *{{Nihongo|[[Umashi-Ashikabi-Hikoji|Umashiashikabihikoji]]|宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神}}<ref name=“Umashiashikabihikoji”>[https://www.japanese-wiki-corpus.org/Shinto/Umashiashikabihikoji.html Umashiashikabihikoji]. Japanese Wiki (Corpus).</ref> - Energy *{{Nihongo|[[Ame-no-Tokotachi]]|天之常立神}}<ref name=“Ame-no-Tokotachi”> [https://www.japanese-wiki-corpus.org/Shinto/Ame-no-Tokotachi%20no%20Kami.html Ame-no-Tokotachi]. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotoamatsukami]][[es:Kotoamatsukami]][[fa:کوتوماتسوکامی]][[fr:Kotoamatsukami]][[hy:Կոտոամացուկամի]][[it:Kotoamatsukami]][[ja:別天津神]][[ko:고토아마쓰카미]][[la:Kotoamatsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯣꯇꯣꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Kotoamatsukami]][[pt:Kotoamatsukami]][[ru:Котоамацуками]][[zh:別天津神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotoamatsukami]][[es:Kotoamatsukami]][[fa:کوتوماتسوکامی]][[fr:Kotoamatsukami]][[hy:Կոտոամացուկամի]][[it:Kotoamatsukami]][[ja:別天津神]][[ko:고토아마쓰카미]][[la:Kotoamatsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯣꯇꯣꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Kotoamatsukami]][[pt:Kotoamatsukami]][[ru:Котоамацуками]][[zh:別天津神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotoamatsukami]][[es:Kotoamatsukami]][[fa:کوتوماتسوکامی]][[fr:Kotoamatsukami]][[hy:Կոտոամացուկամի]][[it:Kotoamatsukami]][[ja:別天津神]][[ko:고토아마쓰카미]][[la:Kotoamatsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯣꯇꯣꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Kotoamatsukami]][[pt:Kotoamatsukami]][[ru:Котоамацуками]][[zh:別天津神]]
{{Short description|Collective name for the first Shinto gods after the creation of the universe}} {{Refimprove|date=March 2017}} [[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg|frame]] In [[Shinto]], {{Nihongo||別天神|'''Kotoamatsukami'''|extra=literally "distinguishing heavenly ''[[kami]]''"}} is the collective name for the first gods which came into existence at the time of the [[Japanese creation myth|creation]] of the {{ill|Universe|en|Universe|WD=Q1|lt=Universe}}. They were born in [[Takamagahara]], the world of Heaven at the time of the creation. Unlike the later gods, these deities were born without any procreation.
The three deities that first appeared were: *{{Nihongo|[[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]|天之御中主神}} - Central Master *{{Nihongo|[[Takamimusubi]]|高御産巣日神|1=Takamimusubi|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} - High Creator *{{Nihongo|[[Kamimusubi]]|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami}} - Divine Creator A bit later, two more deities came into existence: *{{Nihongo|[[Umashi-Ashikabi-Hikoji|Umashiashikabihikoji]]|宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神}}<ref name=“Umashiashikabihikoji”>[https://www.japanese-wiki-corpus.org/Shinto/Umashiashikabihikoji.html Umashiashikabihikoji]. Japanese Wiki (Corpus).</ref> - Energy *{{Nihongo|[[Ame-no-Tokotachi]]|天之常立神}}<ref name=“Ame-no-Tokotachi”> [https://www.japanese-wiki-corpus.org/Shinto/Ame-no-Tokotachi%20no%20Kami.html Ame-no-Tokotachi]. Japanese Wiki (Corpus).</ref> - Heaven The next generation of gods that followed was the [[Kamiyonanayo]], which included [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami}}, the patriarch and matriarch of all other Japanese gods, respectively. Afterward, the Kotoamatsukami "hides away" as ''{{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|WD=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami}}''.<ref>''[[Kojiki]]'', First volume</ref>
Though the Zōkasanshin (three deity of creation) are thought to be genderless, another theory stated Kamimusuhi was the woman and Takamimusubi the man, comparing them with water and fire or with {{ill|Yin and yang|en|Yin and yang|WD=Q62744|lt=Yin and yang}}.
The theologian [[Hirata Atsutane]] identified Amenominakanushi as the spirit of the [[:en:North Star|North Star]], master of the seven stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|en|Big Dipper|WD=Q10460|lt=Big Dipper}}.<ref name="Amenominakanushi">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=38 Amenominakanushi]. Encyclopedia of Shinto.</ref>
Strangely, Takamimusubi later reappeared together with [[Amaterasu]] as one of the central gods in Takamagahara, and his daughter was the mother of the god [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]. He also played important roles in the events of the founding of Japan, such as selecting the gods who would tag along with Ninigi and sending the {{ill|Yatagarasu|en|Yatagarasu|WD=Q745463|lt=Yatagarasu}}, the three legged solar crow, to help {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu}}, who in turn, greatly worshipped him by playing the role of medium priest taking Takami Musubi's identity, in the ceremonies before his [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|Imperial Enthronement]]. Later, Takamimusubi was worshipped by the [[Department of Divinities]] and considered the god of matchmaking. Some Japanese clans also claimed descent from this god, such as the Saeki clan, he is also an Imperial ancestor.
As for Kamimusuhi, he (or she) has strong ties with both the [[Amatsukami]] (heavenly gods) and the Kunitsukami (earthly gods) of Izumo mythology. Kamimusuhi is also said to have transformed the grains produced by the food goddess {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ōgetsuhime}} (Ukemochi no kami) after she was slain by Amaterasu's angered brother.
==See also== *[[Japanese creation myth]] ** {{ill|Creation myth|en|Creation myth|WD=Q215304|lt=Creation myth}} *[[Japanese mythology]]
==Notes== <references/>

{{Short description|Collective name for the first Shinto gods after the creation of the universe}} {{Refimprove|date=March 2017}} [[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg|frame]] In [[Shinto]], {{Nihongo||別天神|'''Kotoamatsukami'''|extra=literally "distinguishing heavenly ''[[kami]]''"}} is the collective name for the first gods which came into existence at the time of the [[Japanese creation myth|creation]] of the {{ill|Universe|en|Universe|WD=Q1|lt=Universe|1=Universe}}. They were born in [[Takamagahara]], the world of Heaven at the time of the creation. Unlike the later gods, these deities were born without any procreation.
The three deities that first appeared were: *{{Nihongo|[[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]|天之御中主神}} - Central Master *{{Nihongo|[[Takamimusubi]]|高御産巣日神|1=Takamimusubi|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} - High Creator *{{Nihongo|[[Kamimusubi]]|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|WD=Q3275901|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami}} - Divine Creator A bit later, two more deities came into existence: *{{Nihongo|[[Umashi-Ashikabi-Hikoji|Umashiashikabihikoji]]|宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神}}<ref name=“Umashiashikabihikoji”>[https://www.japanese-wiki-corpus.org/Shinto/Umashiashikabihikoji.html Umashiashikabihikoji]. Japanese Wiki (Corpus).</ref> - Energy *{{Nihongo|[[Ame-no-Tokotachi]]|天之常立神}}<ref name=“Ame-no-Tokotachi”> [https://www.japanese-wiki-corpus.org/Shinto/Ame-no-Tokotachi%20no%20Kami.html Ame-no-Tokotachi]. Japanese Wiki (Corpus).</ref> - Heaven The next generation of gods that followed was the [[Kamiyonanayo]], which included [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}, the patriarch and matriarch of all other Japanese gods, respectively. Afterward, the Kotoamatsukami "hides away" as ''{{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|WD=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}}''.<ref>''[[Kojiki]]'', First volume</ref>
Though the Zōkasanshin (three deity of creation) are thought to be genderless, another theory stated Kamimusuhi was the woman and Takamimusubi the man, comparing them with water and fire or with {{ill|Yin and yang|en|Yin and yang|WD=Q62744|lt=Yin and yang|1=Yin and yang}}.
The theologian [[Hirata Atsutane]] identified Amenominakanushi as the spirit of the [[:en:North Star|North Star]], master of the seven stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|en|Big Dipper|WD=Q10460|lt=Big Dipper|1=Big Dipper}}.<ref name="Amenominakanushi">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=38 Amenominakanushi]. Encyclopedia of Shinto.</ref>
Strangely, Takamimusubi later reappeared together with [[Amaterasu]] as one of the central gods in Takamagahara, and his daughter was the mother of the god [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]. He also played important roles in the events of the founding of Japan, such as selecting the gods who would tag along with Ninigi and sending the {{ill|Yatagarasu|en|Yatagarasu|WD=Q745463|lt=Yatagarasu|1=Yatagarasu}}, the three legged solar crow, to help {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, who in turn, greatly worshipped him by playing the role of medium priest taking Takami Musubi's identity, in the ceremonies before his [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|Imperial Enthronement]]. Later, Takamimusubi was worshipped by the [[Department of Divinities]] and considered the god of matchmaking. Some Japanese clans also claimed descent from this god, such as the Saeki clan, he is also an Imperial ancestor.
As for Kamimusuhi, he (or she) has strong ties with both the [[Amatsukami]] (heavenly gods) and the Kunitsukami (earthly gods) of Izumo mythology. Kamimusuhi is also said to have transformed the grains produced by the food goddess {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ōgetsuhime|1=Ukemochi}} (Ukemochi no kami) after she was slain by Amaterasu's angered brother.
==See also== *[[Japanese creation myth]] ** {{ill|Creation myth|en|Creation myth|WD=Q215304|lt=Creation myth|1=Creation myth}} *[[Japanese mythology]]
==Notes== <references/>

Afterward, the Kotoamatsukami "hides away" as ''{{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|WD=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}}''.<ref>''[[Kojiki]]'', First volume</ref>
Though the Zōkasanshin (three deity of creation) are thought to be genderless, another theory stated Kamimusuhi was the woman and Takamimusubi the man, comparing them with water and fire or with {{ill|Yin and yang|en|Yin and yang|WD=Q62744|lt=Yin and yang|1=Yin and yang}}.
The theologian [[Hirata Atsutane]] identified Amenominakanushi as the spirit of the [[:en:North Star|North Star]], master of the seven stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|en|Big Dipper|WD=Q10460|lt=Big Dipper|1=Big Dipper}}.<ref name="Amenominakanushi">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=38 Amenominakanushi]. Encyclopedia of Shinto.</ref>
Strangely, Takamimusubi later reappeared together with [[Amaterasu]] as one of the central gods in Takamagahara, and his daughter was the mother of the god [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]. He also played important roles in the events of the founding of Japan, such as selecting the gods who would tag along with Ninigi and sending the {{ill|Yatagarasu|en|Yatagarasu|WD=Q745463|lt=Yatagarasu|1=Yatagarasu}}, the three legged solar crow, to help {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, who in turn, greatly worshipped him by playing the role of medium priest taking Takami Musubi's identity, in the ceremonies before his [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|Imperial Enthronement]]. Later, Takamimusubi was worshipped by the [[Department of Divinities]] and considered the god of matchmaking. Some Japanese clans also claimed descent from this god, such as the Saeki clan, he is also an Imperial ancestor.
As for Kamimusuhi, he (or she) has strong ties with both the [[Amatsukami]] (heavenly gods) and the Kunitsukami (earthly gods) of Izumo mythology. Kamimusuhi is also said to have transformed the grains produced by the food goddess {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ōgetsuhime|1=Ukemochi}} (Ukemochi no kami) after she was slain by Amaterasu's angered brother.
==See also== *[[Japanese creation myth]] ** {{ill|Creation myth|en|Creation myth|WD=Q215304|lt=Creation myth|1=Creation myth}} *[[Japanese mythology]]
==Notes== <references/>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotoamatsukami]][[es:Kotoamatsukami]][[fa:کوتوماتسوکامی]][[fr:Kotoamatsukami]][[hy:Կոտոամացուկամի]][[it:Kotoamatsukami]][[ja:別天津神]][[ko:고토아마쓰카미]][[la:Kotoamatsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯣꯇꯣꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Kotoamatsukami]][[pt:Kotoamatsukami]][[ru:Котоамацуками]][[zh:別天津神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotoamatsukami]][[es:Kotoamatsukami]][[fa:کوتوماتسوکامی]][[fr:Kotoamatsukami]][[hy:Կոտոամացուկամի]][[it:Kotoamatsukami]][[ja:別天津神]][[ko:고토아마쓰카미]][[la:Kotoamatsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯣꯇꯣꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Kotoamatsukami]][[pt:Kotoamatsukami]][[ru:Котоамацуками]][[zh:別天津神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotoamatsukami]][[es:Kotoamatsukami]][[fa:کوتوماتسوکامی]][[fr:Kotoamatsukami]][[hy:Կոտոամացուկամի]][[it:Kotoamatsukami]][[ja:別天津神]][[ko:고토아마쓰카미]][[la:Kotoamatsukami]][[mni:ꯀꯣꯇꯣꯑꯃꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ]][[pl:Kotoamatsukami]][[pt:Kotoamatsukami]][[ru:Котоамацуками]][[zh:別天津神]]
{{Short description|Collective name for the first Shinto gods after the creation of the universe}} {{Refimprove|date=March 2017}} [[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg|frame]] In [[Shinto]], {{Nihongo||別天神|'''Kotoamatsukami'''|extra=literally "distinguishing heavenly ''[[kami]]''"}} is the collective name for the first gods which came into existence at the time of the [[Japanese creation myth|creation]] of the {{ill|Universe|en|Universe|WD=Q1|lt=Universe|1=Universe}}. They were born in [[Takamagahara]], the world of Heaven at the time of the creation. Unlike the later gods, these deities were born without any procreation.
The three deities that first appeared were: *{{Nihongo|[[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]|天之御中主神}} - Central Master *{{Nihongo|[[Takamimusubi]]|高御産巣日神|1=Takamimusubi|WD=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} - High Creator *{{Nihongo|[[Kamimusubi]]|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|WD=Q3275901|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami}} - Divine Creator A bit later, two more deities came into existence: *{{Nihongo|[[Umashi-Ashikabi-Hikoji|Umashiashikabihikoji]]|宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神}}<ref name=“Umashiashikabihikoji”>[https://www.japanese-wiki-corpus.org/Shinto/Umashiashikabihikoji.html Umashiashikabihikoji]. Japanese Wiki (Corpus).</ref> - Energy *{{Nihongo|[[Ame-no-Tokotachi]]|天之常立神}}<ref name=“Ame-no-Tokotachi”> [https://www.japanese-wiki-corpus.org/Shinto/Ame-no-Tokotachi%20no%20Kami.html Ame-no-Tokotachi]. Japanese Wiki (Corpus).</ref> - Heaven The next generation of gods that followed was the [[Kamiyonanayo]], which included [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}, the patriarch and matriarch of all other Japanese gods, respectively. Afterward, the Kotoamatsukami "hides away" as ''{{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|WD=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}}''.<ref>''[[Kojiki]]'', First volume</ref>
Though the Zōkasanshin (three deity of creation) are thought to be genderless, another theory stated Kamimusuhi was the woman and Takamimusubi the man, comparing them with water and fire or with {{ill|Yin and yang|en|Yin and yang|WD=Q62744|lt=Yin and yang|1=Yin and yang}}.
The theologian [[Hirata Atsutane]] identified Amenominakanushi as the spirit of the [[:en:North Star|North Star]], master of the seven stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|en|Big Dipper|WD=Q10460|lt=Big Dipper|1=Big Dipper}}.<ref name="Amenominakanushi">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=38 Amenominakanushi]. Encyclopedia of Shinto.</ref>
Strangely, Takamimusubi later reappeared together with [[Amaterasu]] as one of the central gods in Takamagahara, and his daughter was the mother of the god [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]. He also played important roles in the events of the founding of Japan, such as selecting the gods who would tag along with Ninigi and sending the {{ill|Yatagarasu|en|Yatagarasu|WD=Q745463|lt=Yatagarasu|1=Yatagarasu}}, the three legged solar crow, to help {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|WD=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, who in turn, greatly worshipped him by playing the role of medium priest taking Takami Musubi's identity, in the ceremonies before his [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|Imperial Enthronement]]. Later, Takamimusubi was worshipped by the [[Department of Divinities]] and considered the god of matchmaking. Some Japanese clans also claimed descent from this god, such as the Saeki clan, he is also an Imperial ancestor.
As for Kamimusuhi, he (or she) has strong ties with both the [[Amatsukami]] (heavenly gods) and the Kunitsukami (earthly gods) of Izumo mythology. Kamimusuhi is also said to have transformed the grains produced by the food goddess {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ōgetsuhime|1=Ukemochi}} (Ukemochi no kami) after she was slain by Amaterasu's angered brother.
==See also== *[[Japanese creation myth]] ** {{ill|Creation myth|en|Creation myth|WD=Q215304|lt=Creation myth|1=Creation myth}} *[[Japanese mythology]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Collective name for the first Shinto gods after the creation of the universe}} {{Refimprove|date=March 2017}} [[File:Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg|frame]] In [[Shinto]], {{Nihongo||別天神|'''Kotoamatsukami'''|extra=literally "distinguishing heavenly ''[[kami]]''"}} is the collective name for the first gods which came into existence at the time of the [[Japanese creation myth|creation]] of the {{ill|Universe|en|Universe|qid=Q1|lt=Universe|1=Universe}}. They were born in [[Takamagahara]], the world of Heaven at the time of the creation. Unlike the later gods, these deities were born without any procreation.
The three deities that first appeared were: *{{Nihongo|[[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]|天之御中主神}} - Central Master *{{Nihongo|[[Takamimusubi]]|高御産巣日神|1=Takamimusubi|qid=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi-no-Kami}} - High Creator *{{Nihongo|[[Kamimusubi]]|神産巣日神|1=Kamimusubi|qid=Q3275901|lt=Kamimusubi-no-Kami}} - Divine Creator A bit later, two more deities came into existence: *{{Nihongo|[[Umashi-Ashikabi-Hikoji|Umashiashikabihikoji]]|宇摩志阿斯訶備比古遅神}}<ref name=“Umashiashikabihikoji”>[https://www.japanese-wiki-corpus.org/Shinto/Umashiashikabihikoji.html Umashiashikabihikoji]. Japanese Wiki (Corpus).</ref> - Energy *{{Nihongo|[[Ame-no-Tokotachi]]|天之常立神}}<ref name=“Ame-no-Tokotachi”> [https://www.japanese-wiki-corpus.org/Shinto/Ame-no-Tokotachi%20no%20Kami.html Ame-no-Tokotachi]. Japanese Wiki (Corpus).</ref> - Heaven The next generation of gods that followed was the [[Kamiyonanayo]], which included [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}, the patriarch and matriarch of all other Japanese gods, respectively. Afterward, the Kotoamatsukami "hides away" as ''{{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|qid=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}}''.<ref>''[[Kojiki]]'', First volume</ref>
Though the Zōkasanshin (three deity of creation) are thought to be genderless, another theory stated Kamimusuhi was the woman and Takamimusubi the man, comparing them with water and fire or with {{ill|Yin and yang|en|Yin and yang|qid=Q62744|lt=Yin and yang|1=Yin and yang}}.
The theologian [[Hirata Atsutane]] identified Amenominakanushi as the spirit of the [[:en:North Star|North Star]], master of the seven stars of the {{ill|Big Dipper|en|Big Dipper|qid=Q10460|lt=Big Dipper|1=Big Dipper}}.<ref name="Amenominakanushi">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=38 Amenominakanushi]. Encyclopedia of Shinto.</ref>
Strangely, Takamimusubi later reappeared together with [[Amaterasu]] as one of the central gods in Takamagahara, and his daughter was the mother of the god [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto]]. He also played important roles in the events of the founding of Japan, such as selecting the gods who would tag along with Ninigi and sending the {{ill|Yatagarasu|en|Yatagarasu|qid=Q745463|lt=Yatagarasu|1=Yatagarasu}}, the three legged solar crow, to help {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, who in turn, greatly worshipped him by playing the role of medium priest taking Takami Musubi's identity, in the ceremonies before his [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|Imperial Enthronement]]. Later, Takamimusubi was worshipped by the [[Department of Divinities]] and considered the god of matchmaking. Some Japanese clans also claimed descent from this god, such as the Saeki clan, he is also an Imperial ancestor.
As for Kamimusuhi, he (or she) has strong ties with both the [[Amatsukami]] (heavenly gods) and the Kunitsukami (earthly gods) of Izumo mythology. Kamimusuhi is also said to have transformed the grains produced by the food goddess {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|qid=Q2280323|lt=Ōgetsuhime|1=Ukemochi}} (Ukemochi no kami) after she was slain by Amaterasu's angered brother.
==See also== *[[Japanese creation myth]] ** {{ill|Creation myth|en|Creation myth|qid=Q215304|lt=Creation myth|1=Creation myth}} *[[Japanese mythology]]
